what:
A FHIR adaptor for OpenFn. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to FHIR (generic).

It exposes 5 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. Vendor homepage: https://hl7.org/fhir/.
connect:
HL7 supports bearer tokens, HTTP Basic auth, or smart-on-fhir for API access.

The credential schema asks for baseUrl (required), apiPath (required), username, password, and access_token. Point the credential at the base URL that matches your environment: https://hapi.fhir.org/baseR4, https://server.fire.ly. Auth reference: https://hl7.org/fhir/security.html.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in FHIR (generic).

Triggers haven't been catalogued yet — drive the workflow from a cron schedule or an upstream webhook in the meantime. HL7 can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://hl7.org/fhir/subscription.html), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Drive the workflow from a cron schedule or an upstream webhook trigger. Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in HL7. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running HL7 jobs at volume:

- Prefer HL7 webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Paginate list and search calls explicitly; never assume a single response is the full result set.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: Server-agnostic; choose a baseUrl per deployment. See fhir-4 for an R4-pinned variant.
best:
- Store HL7 credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-fhir to v5.0.17 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Add defensive checks before transforming inbound payloads — vendor responses change between versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
